topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth, once a comforting memory, now felt like a suffocating trap. Elias kept glancing toward the edge of the property, his eyes darting over the dense line of pines that bordered his yard. He’d planted that oak himself, a clumsy, hopeful endeavor when he was ten. Now, where the massive silhouette should have been, there was only a stark, freshly disturbed patch of mud and splintered wood. A cold knot tightened in his stomach. He checked the fence line again, running a hand over the weathered slats as if searching for a hidden wire or a misplaced tool. Every rustle in the undergrowth sounded like heavy boots. He started locking windows, double-checking the deadbolt, the silence of the house amplifying the frantic drumming in his chest. Someone knew. They had to know.

[story 2]

“It can’t be,” Sarah whispered, her voice thin and reedy in the twilight. She knelt beside the bare ground where the sapling had stood, her fingers tracing the shallow, uneven cut in the soil. The air felt thick, charged with unspoken threats. She kept turning her head, listening intently for any sound that wasn't the wind—a distant engine, a twig snapping too close. Her breath hitched, shallow and quick. She pulled her hands away, the chill of the night seeping into her bones despite the mild evening. She looked up at the dark canopy of the neighboring woods, feeling as though unseen eyes were cataloging her every move. The memory of that small, hopeful tree felt like a beacon, now illuminated by a terrifying suspicion.

[story 3]

Mark paced the perimeter of his garden, his movements jerky and sharp. He kept stopping to stare at the precise angle of the missing stump, his jaw tight enough to crack. The shadows lengthened, stretching the familiar shapes of the hedges into menacing figures. He didn't bother turning on the porch light; the darkness seemed preferable, a cloak against whatever was lurking just beyond the reach of his sight. He muttered to himself, a low, agitated stream of words that barely disturbed the quiet. He examined the disturbed soil again, pressing his palm flat against the disturbed earth, searching for any sign—a footprint, a discarded piece of twine—anything to confirm the terrifying implication swirling in his mind. The stillness of the night was deceptive; it felt expectant, waiting for him to make the wrong move.
